About: It is about the face of Jesus Christ.
It is about the Keramidion, a holy tile.
It is about the Image of Edessa.
AssociatedWith: It is associated with the Georgian monastery of Ancha.
It is associated with the National Art Museum of Georgia.
It is associated with the Anchiskhati church.
It is associated with the church of the Nativity of the Theotokos in Tbilisi.
ConsistsOf: It consists of a chased silver riza.
It consists of a middle panel of a triptych.
It consists of gilded chasing.
It consists of lateral leaves depicting scenes from the New Testament.
It consists of the face of the Savior.
It consists of Christ Pantocrator.
It consists of the bust of Jesus.
It consists of the scene of deesis.
It consists of archangels Michael and Gabriel.
It consists of apostles Peter and John.
CreatedAround: It was created around the 6th-7th century.
CreatedIn: It was created in the 6th-7th century.
HasA: It has a chased silver riza.
It has a kiot, an icon box.
HasFeatureOf: It is characterized by being a medieval Georgian encaustic icon.
It is characterized by being a holy tile imprinted with the face of Jesus Christ.
It is characterized by being miraculously transferred by contact with the Image of Edessa.
It is characterized by being partly repainted in the following centuries.
It is characterized by its name derived from the Georgian monastery of Ancha.
It is characterized by its acquisition by the Georgian catholicos Domenti III.
It is characterized by its veneration as one of the most venerated relics of Georgian Christianity.
It is characterized by its identification with the Keramidion.
It is characterized by its association with the Image of Edessa.
It is characterized by its preservation from the iconoclastic campaign by the Byzantine emperor Leo III the Isaurian.
HeldIn: It was held in the National Art Museum of Georgia.
It was held in the church of the Nativity of the Theotokos in Tbilisi.
InfluencedBy: It was influenced by the Image of Edessa.
It was influenced by the Mandylion.
It was influenced by the Byzantine emperor Leo III the Isaurian.
IsA: It is a medieval Georgian encaustic icon.
It is a work of medieval Georgian art.
KnownAs: It is known as the Keramidion.
It is known as the Image of Edessa.
It is known as the Mandylion.
KnownFor: It is known for being a holy tile imprinted with the face of Jesus Christ miraculously transferred by contact with the Image of Edessa.
It is known for its origin from the Georgian monastery of Ancha.
It is known for its acquisition by the Georgian catholicos Domenti III.
It is known for being one of the most venerated relics of Georgian Christianity.
LocatedIn: It is located in the National Art Museum of Georgia.
MemberOf: It is a member of Georgian Christianity.
NamedAfter: It is named after the Georgian monastery of Ancha.
OccurredDuring: It occurred during the iconoclastic campaign by the Byzantine emperor Leo III the Isaurian.
OccurredIn: It occurred in the Georgian princedom of Klarjeti.
OriginatedIn: It originated in the Georgian monastery of Ancha.
PartOf: It is part of the National Art Museum of Georgia.
It is part of the church of the Nativity of the Theotokos in Tbilisi.
It is part of the Georgian princedom of Samtskhe.
It is part of the Georgian princedom of Klarjeti.
UsedAs: It is used as a holy tile.
It is used as a relic.
UsedFor: It is used for preserving the face of Christ.
UsedIn: It is used in the National Art Museum of Georgia.
It is used in the church of the Nativity of the Theotokos in Tbilisi.
UsedTo: It is used to preserve the face of Christ from the iconoclastic campaign.
